narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 47% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction is based on a careful look at key parts of your academic and background profile, like your LSAT score of 32, which is below the typical 36.7 average, your undergraduate GPA of 2.7 compared to the usual 3.21, and your school deciles of 1 for both first-year and third-year grades—much lower than the averages around 5.7 and 5.5. Your middle family income bracket and Asian racial background also factored in, as they differ from the most common profiles in our data, where White students make up over 83% and incomes vary more widely. Being female and planning full-time enrollment played smaller roles overall. The strongest influence pushing the model toward predicting bar failure was your LSAT score, as it stood out as the top factor in the decision. Right behind it were your third-year grades decile, then your first-year grades decile, each significantly weighing against a passing prediction because they were in the lowest ranks compared to peers. Your family income and racial background came next in influence, subtly nudging the odds lower, followed by your undergrad GPA. Gender had even less sway, and your full-time status actually provided a tiny positive boost toward expecting success. Overall, your profile showed weaker academic signals than most admitted students, tipping the balance toward caution on bar passage risk. To improve your chances next time, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to aim above the average, strengthen your GPA through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and build a stronger high school or early college grade record if possible. These steps could shift those key factors in your favor and better align you with successful bar takers. We encourage reapplying after addressing these areas—many students turn things around with targeted effort. Keep pushing forward; your determination will make a difference.
